texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Managing your food and fluid intake when on haemodialysis
texts.text: Managing your food and fluid intake when on haemodialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oxford Kidney Unit
texts.text: Oxford Kidney Unit

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 2
texts.text: Page 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you are on haemodialysis this leaflet will help you manage your food and fluid intake. Your dietitian will also be able to answer any questions or concerns.
texts.text: If you are on haemodialysis this leaflet will help you manage your food and fluid intake. Your dietitian will also be able to answer any questions or concerns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When your kidneys are not working properly, waste products build up in your blood. Haemodialysis works by removing these waste products and excess fluid. However, it cannot completely replace the work of normal kidneys. Therefore, to stay well it will be necessary to alter some of the foods and drinks you usually have.
texts.text: When your kidneys are not working properly, waste products build up in your blood. Haemodialysis works by removing these waste products and excess fluid. However, it cannot completely replace the work of normal kidneys. Therefore, to stay well it will be necessary to alter some of the foods and drinks you usually have.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dietitians will look at the following in your diet:
texts.text: The dietitians will look at the following in your diet:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  protein
texts.text: protein

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  salt
texts.text: salt

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  fluid
texts.text: fluid

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  potassium
texts.text: potassium

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  phosphate
texts.text: phosphate

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  fibre.
texts.text: fibre.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 3
texts.text: Page 3

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Protein
texts.text: Protein

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Protein is needed for the growth and repair of all body tissues. This includes maintaining your muscle strength.
texts.text: Protein is needed for the growth and repair of all body tissues. This includes maintaining your muscle strength.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A small amount of protein is lost during the dialysis process. Therefore, to ensure that you have enough protein in your diet you should aim to eat high protein foods at least twice a day .
texts.text: A small amount of protein is lost during the dialysis process. Therefore, to ensure that you have enough protein in your diet you should aim to eat high protein foods at least twice a day .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The following foods that are good sources of protein to include as part of your meals:
texts.text: The following foods that are good sources of protein to include as part of your meals:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  meat, poultry
texts.text: meat, poultry

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  fish
texts.text: fish

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  soya protein products, Quorn, tofu, pea protein products
texts.text: soya protein products, Quorn, tofu, pea protein products

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  lentils, pulses
texts.text: lentils, pulses

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  eggs.
texts.text: eggs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These foods also contain protein, but they are higher in phosphate so may have to be limited (see the phosphate section on page 10):
texts.text: These foods also contain protein, but they are higher in phosphate so may have to be limited (see the phosphate section on page 10):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  milk
texts.text: milk

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  cheese
texts.text: cheese

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  yogurt.
texts.text: yogurt.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 4
texts.text: Page 4

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Salt
texts.text: Salt

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We would advise you to reduce salt in your diet. Reducing salt can help:
texts.text: We would advise you to reduce salt in your diet. Reducing salt can help:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Reduce feelings of thirst (useful if you are on a fluid restriction).
texts.text: Reduce feelings of thirst (useful if you are on a fluid restriction).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Prevent carrying extra fluid (oedema).
texts.text: Prevent carrying extra fluid (oedema).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Reduce blood pressure.
texts.text: Reduce blood pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some simple ways of reducing your salt intake include:
texts.text: Some simple ways of reducing your salt intake include:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Avoid or using less salt in cooking and avoid adding salt at the table (this includes sea salt, pink Himalayan salt as well as table salt).
texts.text: Avoid or using less salt in cooking and avoid adding salt at the table (this includes sea salt, pink Himalayan salt as well as table salt).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Use alternative flavourings such as herbs, spices, black pepper, garlic, lemon juice to flavour your food.
texts.text: Use alternative flavourings such as herbs, spices, black pepper, garlic, lemon juice to flavour your food.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Reducing your intake of foods which are high in salt and switching to lower salt alternatives.
texts.text: Reducing your intake of foods which are high in salt and switching to lower salt alternatives.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: List of high and lower salt alternative foods
texts.text: List of high and lower salt alternative foods

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 5
texts.text: Page 5

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: DAIRY Higher salt foods
texts.text: DAIRY Higher salt foods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cheese spread (such as Dairylea, Primula), hard cheeses (such as cheddar, edam, stilton, halloumi)
texts.text: Cheese spread (such as Dairylea, Primula), hard cheeses (such as cheddar, edam, stilton, halloumi)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SAVOURY SNACKS Higher salt foods
texts.text: SAVOURY SNACKS Higher salt foods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Crisps, salted nuts, salted snacks (such as Twiglets, Pretzels), Ritz biscuits, TUC biscuits, mini cheddars
texts.text: Crisps, salted nuts, salted snacks (such as Twiglets, Pretzels), Ritz biscuits, TUC biscuits, mini cheddars

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: MISCELLANEOUS Higher salt foods
texts.text: MISCELLANEOUS Higher salt foods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Marmite, Bovril, stock cubes, gravy granules, tinned or packet soups, Pot Noodles, bottled sauces such as soy sauce and tomato ketchup
texts.text: Marmite, Bovril, stock cubes, gravy granules, tinned or packet soups, Pot Noodles, bottled sauces such as soy sauce and tomato ketchup

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Lower salt alternatives
texts.text: Lower salt alternatives

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cottage cheese, cream cheese (such as Philadelphia), mozzarella
texts.text: Cottage cheese, cream cheese (such as Philadelphia), mozzarella

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Lower salt alternatives
texts.text: Lower salt alternatives

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Plain popcorn, plain breadsticks, cream crackers, Ryvita, rice cakes
texts.text: Plain popcorn, plain breadsticks, cream crackers, Ryvita, rice cakes

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Lower salt alternatives
texts.text: Lower salt alternatives

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Natex savoury spread, Meridian yeast extract, low or zero salt stock cubes, reduced salt gravy granules or homemade gravy, homemade soup, reduced salt sauces, vinegar
texts.text: Natex savoury spread, Meridian yeast extract, low or zero salt stock cubes, reduced salt gravy granules or homemade gravy, homemade soup, reduced salt sauces, vinegar

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you have been advised to follow a low-potassium diet, you should avoid all salt substitutes such as LoSalt and SoLo as they contain potassium chloride.
texts.text: If you have been advised to follow a low-potassium diet, you should avoid all salt substitutes such as LoSalt and SoLo as they contain potassium chloride.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 6
texts.text: Page 6

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Understanding food labelling
texts.text: Understanding food labelling

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Approximately 75% of our salt intake comes from ready-made foods. It is important to look at food labels when making decisions about which foods to buy. Remember, food labels may show the salt content per 100g and not always the salt content of the entire portion.
texts.text: Approximately 75% of our salt intake comes from ready-made foods. It is important to look at food labels when making decisions about which foods to buy. Remember, food labels may show the salt content per 100g and not always the salt content of the entire portion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Choose foods with more green coding and fewer with amber coding. Where possible try to avoid foods with red coding.
texts.text: Choose foods with more green coding and fewer with amber coding. Where possible try to avoid foods with red coding.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 7
texts.text: Page 7

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Fluid
texts.text: Fluid

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When your kidneys are not working properly you are not able to get rid of all the fluid that you drink. Haemodialysis helps to remove fluid from the body, but if you drink more than is being removed you can become fluid overloaded.
texts.text: When your kidneys are not working properly you are not able to get rid of all the fluid that you drink. Haemodialysis helps to remove fluid from the body, but if you drink more than is being removed you can become fluid overloaded.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The signs of being fluid overloaded are a rapid increase in weight between dialysis sessions and a rise in blood pressure. It may also cause swollen ankles and breathlessness.
texts.text: The signs of being fluid overloaded are a rapid increase in weight between dialysis sessions and a rise in blood pressure. It may also cause swollen ankles and breathlessness.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Your fluid allowance =   500ml + previous days 24-hour urine output
texts.text: Your fluid allowance =   500ml + previous days 24-hour urine output

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most people on dialysis pass less urine over time; if this happens you will need to have your fluid allowance reassessed and will need to reduce your fluid intake. Ask your haemodialysis nurse or dietitian for more information.
texts.text: Most people on dialysis pass less urine over time; if this happens you will need to have your fluid allowance reassessed and will need to reduce your fluid intake. Ask your haemodialysis nurse or dietitian for more information.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What counts as fluid?
texts.text: What counts as fluid?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  all hot and cold drinks
texts.text: all hot and cold drinks

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  soup, gravy, thin sauces
texts.text: soup, gravy, thin sauces

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  jelly, ice lollies
texts.text: jelly, ice lollies

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  ice cubes.
texts.text: ice cubes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fluid rich foods such as custard, yoghurt and ice cream count as half the volume of fluid, for example 120ml yoghurt = 60ml fluid.
texts.text: Fluid rich foods such as custard, yoghurt and ice cream count as half the volume of fluid, for example 120ml yoghurt = 60ml fluid.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 8
texts.text: Page 8

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Tips to help you keep to your fluid allowance:
texts.text: Tips to help you keep to your fluid allowance:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Use a measuring jug or water bottle to help manage your allowance.
texts.text: Use a measuring jug or water bottle to help manage your allowance.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •    Use a smaller cup or only fill your cup or mug to halfway.
texts.text: Use a smaller cup or only fill your cup or mug to halfway.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    T ry to drink only when you are thirsty rather than out of habit or to be sociable.
texts.text: T ry to drink only when you are thirsty rather than out of habit or to be sociable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Sucking ice cubes or ice lollies can be more refreshing, use squash to flavour ice cubes.
texts.text: Sucking ice cubes or ice lollies can be more refreshing, use squash to flavour ice cubes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Sucking sugar free sweets such as mints, boiled sweets or chewing gum.
texts.text: Sucking sugar free sweets such as mints, boiled sweets or chewing gum.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •    A piece of fruit can be a refreshing alternative to having a drink.
texts.text: A piece of fruit can be a refreshing alternative to having a drink.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Avoid eating salty foods or adding salt to your foods as these will make you thirsty.
texts.text: Avoid eating salty foods or adding salt to your foods as these will make you thirsty.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For more information, ask your dialysis nurse or dietitian for the managing your fluid balance leaflet.
texts.text: For more information, ask your dialysis nurse or dietitian for the managing your fluid balance leaflet.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 9
texts.text: Page 9

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Potassium
texts.text: Potassium

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Potassium is a mineral found naturally in foods. Haemodialysis removes potassium from the blood, but levels build up between dialysis sessions. It is therefore often necessary to restrict dietary potassium to avoid levels becoming too high.
texts.text: Potassium is a mineral found naturally in foods. Haemodialysis removes potassium from the blood, but levels build up between dialysis sessions. It is therefore often necessary to restrict dietary potassium to avoid levels becoming too high.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The target potassium level is 3.5-5.9 mmol/ L.
texts.text: The target potassium level is 3.5-5.9 mmol/ L.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A high level of potassium in your blood can be dangerous for your heart. However, the amount of potassium you can include in your diet is very individual and not everyone on haemodialysis needs to follow a low potassium diet.
texts.text: A high level of potassium in your blood can be dangerous for your heart. However, the amount of potassium you can include in your diet is very individual and not everyone on haemodialysis needs to follow a low potassium diet.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here is a table highlighting some foods and drinks that are high in potassium. If you need to reduce your potassium your dietitian can give you guidance and more detailed information.
texts.text: Here is a table highlighting some foods and drinks that are high in potassium. If you need to reduce your potassium your dietitian can give you guidance and more detailed information.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: List of foods that are high in potassium
texts.text: List of foods that are high in potassium

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 10
texts.text: Page 10

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Phosphate
texts.text: Phosphate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Phosphate is a mineral found naturally in foods and is also used as a food additive.
texts.text: Phosphate is a mineral found naturally in foods and is also used as a food additive.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Phosphate levels in the blood can increase when the kidneys are not working properly.
texts.text: Phosphate levels in the blood can increase when the kidneys are not working properly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over time, this can lead to heart disease and weaker bones which are more likely to break. It can also cause your skin and eyes to be itchy.
texts.text: Over time, this can lead to heart disease and weaker bones which are more likely to break. It can also cause your skin and eyes to be itchy.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The target phosphate level is 1.1-1.7mmol/L.
texts.text: The target phosphate level is 1.1-1.7mmol/L.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Haemodialysis removes some phosphate from the blood, but it may be necessary to have less phosphate in your diet as well. You may also have been prescribed a medicine called a phosphate binder, which reduces the amount of phosphate you absorb from your food. Your dietitian can give you more information on when to take this.
texts.text: Haemodialysis removes some phosphate from the blood, but it may be necessary to have less phosphate in your diet as well. You may also have been prescribed a medicine called a phosphate binder, which reduces the amount of phosphate you absorb from your food. Your dietitian can give you more information on when to take this.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here is a list of your individual goals for controlling phosphate:
texts.text: Here is a list of your individual goals for controlling phosphate:

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 11
texts.text: Page 11

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Fibre
texts.text: Fibre

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fibre is important to prevent constipation and keep your bowels regular.
texts.text: Fibre is important to prevent constipation and keep your bowels regular.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Foods high in fibre that are good for you to eat are:
texts.text: Foods high in fibre that are good for you to eat are:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Wholemeal or granary bread instead of white bread.
texts.text: Wholemeal or granary bread instead of white bread.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Whole wheat breakfast cereal such as Weetabix, Shredded Wheat, Bran flakes.
texts.text: Whole wheat breakfast cereal such as Weetabix, Shredded Wheat, Bran flakes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •    Brown rice and wholemeal pasta rather than white.
texts.text: Brown rice and wholemeal pasta rather than white.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Pulses such as lentils, chickpeas, beans, try and include these in a meal once a week.
texts.text: Pulses such as lentils, chickpeas, beans, try and include these in a meal once a week.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •    Fruit and vegetables, aim to eat 5 portions per day.
texts.text: Fruit and vegetables, aim to eat 5 portions per day.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 12
texts.text: Page 12

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Eating when on Dialysis
texts.text: Eating when on Dialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During haemodialysis, it is still important to follow your individual diet. It is a good idea to eat a sandwich or other protein containing snack on dialysis if you can.
texts.text: During haemodialysis, it is still important to follow your individual diet. It is a good idea to eat a sandwich or other protein containing snack on dialysis if you can.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you eat foods that are high in potassium or phosphate during a dialysis session, they won't be absorbed into the body until after your dialysis has finished. This means they will have the same effect on your blood results as eating them on non-dialysis days.
texts.text: If you eat foods that are high in potassium or phosphate during a dialysis session, they won't be absorbed into the body until after your dialysis has finished. This means they will have the same effect on your blood results as eating them on non-dialysis days.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remember to take your phosphate binders to the dialysis unit.
texts.text: Remember to take your phosphate binders to the dialysis unit.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: If you have a poor appetite
texts.text: If you have a poor appetite

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Eat little and often, have small meals with nourishing snacks in between.
texts.text: Eat little and often, have small meals with nourishing snacks in between.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    Cold foods can sometimes be more appetising, try a sandwich or chilled dessert.
texts.text: Cold foods can sometimes be more appetising, try a sandwich or chilled dessert.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·    If there are times of the day when your appetite is better, take advantage of this.
texts.text: If there are times of the day when your appetite is better, take advantage of this.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Contact your renal dietitian or dialysis nurse if your appetite remains reduced and you are losing weight.
texts.text: Contact your renal dietitian or dialysis nurse if your appetite remains reduced and you are losing weight.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Page 13
texts.text: Page 13

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Keeping Active
texts.text: Keeping Active

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When you first start haemodialysis, you may feel tired and lack energy. However, dialysis should not prevent you from being physically active. In fact, it can help with your overall health. Benefits include:
texts.text: When you first start haemodialysis, you may feel tired and lack energy. However, dialysis should not prevent you from being physically active. In fact, it can help with your overall health. Benefits include:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Improved heart health
texts.text: Improved heart health

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Better blood pressure control
texts.text: Better blood pressure control

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Improved sleep
texts.text: Improved sleep

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Increased efficiency of dialysis
texts.text: Increased efficiency of dialysis

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Less cramp on dialysis
texts.text: Less cramp on dialysis

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Helping to maintain strength and balance
texts.text: Helping to maintain strength and balance

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Improving your mood and mental wellbeing.
texts.text: Improving your mood and mental wellbeing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you would like more information on suitable exercises you can ask one of the team for the Trust's information sheet on Chair Based Exercises for Strength and Balance (Information for renal dialysis patients) or visit the MOVE website: www.move.bangor.ac.uk *which includes explanations and videos of activities for those on haemodialysis.
texts.text: If you would like more information on suitable exercises you can ask one of the team for the Trust's information sheet on Chair Based Exercises for Strength and Balance (Information for renal dialysis patients) or visit the MOVE website: www.move.bangor.ac.uk *which includes explanations and videos of activities for those on haemodialysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To keep safe when exercising, remember to always stay within your capabilities, so start slowly and build up steadily, take rests as you need to and do not overdo it.
texts.text: To keep safe when exercising, remember to always stay within your capabilities, so start slowly and build up steadily, take rests as you need to and do not overdo it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you have concerns about performing certain activities with a Tesio or fistula, you should check with your dialysis nurse or doctor to ensure it is safe to do so first.
texts.text: If you have concerns about performing certain activities with a Tesio or fistula, you should check with your dialysis nurse or doctor to ensure it is safe to do so first.

texts.orig: Page 16

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dietitians can only provide advice or information to patients cared for under the Oxford Kidney or Transplant Unit (Churchill hospital). This includes the network units at Banbury, High Wycombe, Milton Keynes, Stoke Mandeville and Swindon (Great Western Hospital). If you do not attend these sites, then please contact your local care team.
texts.text: The dietitians can only provide advice or information to patients cared for under the Oxford Kidney or Transplant Unit (Churchill hospital). This includes the network units at Banbury, High Wycombe, Milton Keynes, Stoke Mandeville and Swindon (Great Western Hospital). If you do not attend these sites, then please contact your local care team.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SCKPA is run for patients by patients or family members.
texts.text: The SCKPA is run for patients by patients or family members.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: They offer support to people suffering from kidney disease or who are on dialysis. They work closely with the Oxford Kidney Unit and have branches in Oxfordshire, Northamptonshire, Buckinghamshire, and Milton Keynes, and parts of Wiltshire, Gloucestershire and Berkshire.
texts.text: They offer support to people suffering from kidney disease or who are on dialysis. They work closely with the Oxford Kidney Unit and have branches in Oxfordshire, Northamptonshire, Buckinghamshire, and Milton Keynes, and parts of Wiltshire, Gloucestershire and Berkshire.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Health for Me (patient portal)
texts.text: Health for Me (patient portal)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Health for Me enables you to access to your digital health record via the OUH Patient Portal. It is an online system that allows you to easily view parts of your digital health record safely and securely from a computer or smartphone.
texts.text: Health for Me enables you to access to your digital health record via the OUH Patient Portal. It is an online system that allows you to easily view parts of your digital health record safely and securely from a computer or smartphone.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Please ask a member of the renal team to sign you up to the patient portal.
texts.text: Please ask a member of the renal team to sign you up to the patient portal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If your bloods are taken by the Oxford Kidney Unit you will be able to see these results. We can give you a leaflet that will help you understand the results. If you would like a copy, please ask a member of the renal team.
texts.text: If your bloods are taken by the Oxford Kidney Unit you will be able to see these results. We can give you a leaflet that will help you understand the results. If you would like a copy, please ask a member of the renal team.
